summary: High-risk regulatory changes for food seasoning/snack compliance: Japan introduced mandatory compliance for General Food Safety and Sanitation. Middle East (GCC/GSO) now requires mandatory allergen declaration for prepackaged foods. Singapore implemented stricter additive rules, including specific limits for Class II preservatives in kuehs (not direct addition), general compliance for all additives/contaminants, prohibition of unpermitted/exceeding limits, and mandatory declaration of ingredients/additives on labels. Taiwan added mandatory labeling for 11 major allergens, including sulphite >= 10 mg/kg. Thailand requires verification and compliance with a list of prohibited foods. The United States introduced new prohibitions for delisted color additives and updated rules for other prohibited substances. Mandatory labeling for nine major food allergens (including sesame), specific tree nut/fish/shellfish types, cochineal extract/carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfiting agents (>=10 ppm) is now required. New guidance/action levels for 3-MCPD (1 ppm) and Aflatoxins (20 ppb) were added, and facilities must implement allergen preventive controls. Moderate-risk additions/modifications include updated guidance for additives and contaminants in Codex, new requirements for additives and microbiological limits in the EU, new rules for Halal food, prepackaged food labeling, expiration dates, and microbiological limits in GCC, new regulations for Dinitrogen Monoxide (N2O) as an additive, export certificates, food categorization, and online food oversight in Indonesia, and updated additive limits, new contaminant rules (agricultural chemical residues, general food), and new labeling requirements (health claims, nutrition, special dietary uses) in Japan. Several moderate-risk rules were also removed across Codex, EU, GCC/GSO, Indonesia, and Japan, requiring verification of source changes.

changes.notes: Compliance with the Food Safety Basic Law is mandatory for all food products.
Compliance with the Food Sanitation Law is mandatory for all food products.

changes.parameter: Declaration of allergens in prepackaged foodstuffs
changes.notes: Specific allergen declaration requirements, including lists and formatting, must be verified against the full GSO 9:2022 technical regulation.
Can only be directly added to fillings and toppings in kuehs.

changes.parameter: Class II chemical preservatives (e.g., sorbic acid, benzoic acid)
changes.newLimit: Not to be directly added during manufacturing

changes.notes: Applies to all food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore.
Applies to all food businesses.
Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat containing these residues.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared on food labels
changes.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish, sulphite
changes.notes: These 11 allergens require mandatory labeling if present as an ingredient or food additive. Sulphite requires labeling if residue sulphur dioxide is 10 mg/kg or more.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited from production, import, or sale. Verification required for specific list.
changes.notes: A list of foods prohibited from production, import, or sale exists and must be verified.
Delisted color additives are in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30).

changes.oldLimit: Prohibited from use in food. Verification required against FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189).
changes.notes: Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189).

changes.newLimit: Milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
changes.notes: These are the nine major food allergens recognized by U.S. law (FALCPA and FASTER Act).
This is a Guidance Level.

changes.newLimit: Facilities must put written procedures in place to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling.
changes.notes: Established by the "Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food" rule (CGMP & PC rule, 21 CFR part 117).

changes.newLimit: Must be declared on the label.
changes.notes: Identified as allergenic substances.

changes.newLimit: Must be specifically labeled with the name of the allergen source.
changes.notes: The name of the food source must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name OR immediately after/next to the ingredient list in a 'contains' statement.
Required due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.

changes.newLimit: Must meet a defined standard for gluten content.
changes.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling.

changes.parameter: Specificity for Tree Nuts, Fish, and Crustacean Shellfish
changes.newLimit: The type of tree nut, and the species of fish and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
changes.notes: Examples: almonds, pecans, walnuts for tree nuts; bass, flounder, cod for fish; crab, lobster, shrimp for Crustacean shellfish.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration is ≥10 total sulfites
changes.notes: Due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals (e.g., asthma).

changes.oldLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
changes.notes: Verification of permitted food additives and their conditions of use is required via the Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) Online Database, which is regularly updated.

changes.parameter: Contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants (e.g., mycotoxins, metals like arsenic, lead, mercury)
changes.notes: Verification of maximum levels (MLs) or guideline levels for contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants is required via the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and related Codex texts.

This regulation outlines the procedures for issuing export certificates for processed foods. Compliance with relevant food safety and quality standards, which may include labelling, is typically a prerequisite for such certificates. The full document must be consulted for detailed procedures.

This regulation defines food categories, which is essential for determining the applicability of other specific regulations regarding additives, contaminants, and labelling for processed foods, including seasoning and snack products. The full document must be consulted for detailed categories.

This regulation covers the oversight of drugs and food distributed online. It likely includes provisions for ensuring that information provided for online sales of processed foods, including labelling details, is accurate and compliant. The full document must be consulted for specific requirements.
